hook: The Grade II* country house where Air Chief Marshal Dowding ran RAF Fighter Command and won the Battle of Britain, now a museum with a recreated Filter Room.
facts: Bentley Priory was Headquarters Fighter Command during the Battle of Britain (1940), from where Air Chief Marshal Sir Hugh Dowding directed the air defence of Britain.
The house is a Grade II* listed building, officially designated on 25 May 1983, set in grounds on the National Register of Historic Parks and Gardens.
Dowding was raised to the peerage as Baron Dowding of Bentley Priory in 1943 in recognition of his command.

The RAF closed the site on 30 May 2008; the Bentley Priory Museum opened to the public in September 2013, officially opened by the Prince of Wales (then Charles) and the Duchess of Cornwall on 12 September 2013.
Exhibits cover the 'Dowding System' of integrated air defence and include a recreated Filter Room where radar and Observer Corps information was plotted.
The museum is run by The Bentley Priory Battle of Britain Trust (postcode HA7 3HT).
w: From this elegant Stanmore mansion, Air Chief Marshal Dowding ran Fighter Command and won the Battle of Britain in 1940, directing 'the Few' from the Filter Room below. Once a stately home and a girls' school, it reopened in 2013 as a museum of that desperate summer. Where the battle was directed, not fought.

hook: Britain's first international airport, birthplace of air traffic control and the 'Mayday' call, with a museum in the world's first purpose-built airport terminal and control tower.
facts: Croydon was Britain's first major international airport and London's principal airport between the wars, predecessor to Heathrow.

The terminal building and control tower (now Airport House, 265 Purley Way, CR0 0XZ) opened in 1928 as the world's first purpose-designed airport terminal and air traffic control tower, officially opened by Lady Maud Hoare on 2 May 1928.
The international distress call 'Mayday' was devised here in 1923 by Croydon radio officer Frederick Stanley Mockford.
The airport officially closed at 22:20 on 30 September 1959, superseded by Heathrow.
Airport House is a Grade II* listed building, surviving as a rare example of the first wave of purpose-built airport terminals.
The Visitor Centre and museum occupy the former control tower and open to the public on the first Sunday of each month; tower access is by pre-booked guided tour.
w: Long before Heathrow, Croydon was Britain's gateway to the world — the first purpose-built international airport, where Amy Johnson took off for Australia and air-traffic control was invented. The airfield closed in 1959, but the 1928 control tower survives as Airport House, with a museum open monthly. Aviation's forgotten cradle.

hook: A roadside archway made of two genuine blue-whale jawbones, marking the drive to a hidden Barnet estate.
facts: Two whale jawbones form an archway at the top of the private drive leading to Whalebones House, off Wood Street between High Barnet and Barnet Hospital (EN5 4BZ).
The current bones were installed in May 1939 by then-owner Gwyneth Cowing, replacing an earlier set of jawbones that had formed a gateway to the house since the 1860s or earlier.

Each jawbone weighs about three-quarters of a ton and they are roughly 24 feet long; they came from a blue whale captured in the South Seas, dismembered in Norway, shipped via Hull and trucked to Wood Street.
It took half a dozen workmen, directed by local builder W. Foster & Sons, a full day to set the bones into about six feet of concrete.
The name 'Whalebones' for the house first appears on a map dated 1872; the house itself was built around 1815.

Since the 1987 death of artist and philanthropist Gwyneth Cowing, the surrounding estate of fields and woods has been run by trustees named in her will; a controversial housing scheme on the land was approved in 2024.
legend: Local tradition holds that it is good luck to walk under the archway, make a wish, or share a kiss beneath it.
w: On a quiet lane in Arkley, two giant blue-whale jawbones arch over the drive to an old estate, a piece of Victorian eccentricity in the Barnet hedgerows. The present bones went up in 1939, replacing an even older pair. Locals say a kiss beneath the arch brings luck. Whale bones in the green belt.

hook: The world's first museum devoted entirely to hand fans, set in a pair of restored Georgian townhouses on Crooms Hill.
facts: The Fan Museum opened in 1991 and is the world's first museum dedicated to the preservation and display of hand fans, at 12 Crooms Hill in the Greenwich World Heritage Site (SE10 8ER).
It occupies two Grade II* listed townhouses built around 1718-1721, which were dilapidated when founders Helene and Anthony 'Dickie' Alexander bought and restored them in the 1980s.
The collection holds over 6,000 fans and fan-related items, with the oldest fan dating from the 11th century and a particularly strong holding of 18th- and 19th-century European fans.
An orangery decorated with hand-painted murals connects the two houses and overlooks a Japanese-style garden with a fan-shaped parterre, a pond and a stream.
The houses had earlier been converted into flats and at one point used as accommodation for medical staff connected to the nearby former Royal Naval Hospital.
w: The world's only museum devoted to the hand fan fills a pair of Georgian townhouses on Greenwich's Crooms Hill, its thousands of folding fans ranging from jewelled court pieces to propaganda in pleated silk. Opened in 1991, it keeps an orangery and a fan-shaped garden. A vanished art of flirtation. Small, exquisite and gloriously niche.

hook: A water-filled medieval moat lost in the woods at the edge of Trent Park, whose name folklore stretches into 'Camelot'.
facts: A quadrangular moated earthwork in Moat Wood, near the summit of Ferny Hill within Trent Country Park, Enfield; the island platform is roughly 69m by 53m and the moat averages about 10m wide.
It is a Scheduled Monument, protected under the Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Areas Act 1979 (Historic England list entry 1005551).

The medieval moat survives as a water-filled earthwork; partial excavation in the 1920s (under Sir Philip Sassoon) and later surface finds recovered roof tiles, a 14th-century green-glazed floor tile and a timber piece dated to 1357.
Access is on foot from the Hadley Road side of Trent Country Park; the site lies in woodland away from the main park facilities.
The name 'Camlet' is recorded locally from the 15th century and gave its name to nearby Camlet Way.
legend: Local folklore holds that 'Camlet' is a corruption of 'Camelot', tying the moat to Arthurian legend as 'London's Camelot'; documents record a 'Manor of Camelot' ordered demolished around 1439-1440, its materials reused at Hertford Castle.

The site is traditionally linked to Geoffrey de Mandeville, 1st Earl of Essex and Constable of the Tower of London, whose ghost is said to haunt the moat and to guard treasure hidden in a well on the island.
w: Deep in the woods of Trent Park lies a square moated island, all that remains of a medieval manor — but the name, a corruption of 'Camelot,' has snagged it on Arthurian legend and tales of treasure guarded by the ghost of Geoffrey de Mandeville. Witches and dowsers still visit. A scrap of Camelot in suburban Enfield.

hook: The lone surviving medieval chancel of Cheam's vanished old church, kept standing to shelter its Tudor tombs.
facts: The chapel is the former chancel of the old parish church of St Dunstan, Cheam; when a new St Dunstan's was built in 1864 the medieval church was demolished except this east end, retained for its monuments and brasses.
Remains of two round-headed windows in the north wall may be pre-Conquest, surviving from the earliest masonry church dedicated to St Dunstan (Archbishop of Canterbury, died 988).

In the 1590s John Lumley, 1st Baron Lumley, converted the chancel into a memorial chapel; it holds alabaster and marble monuments to Lady Jane Fitzalan (d.1578), Lord Lumley (d.1609) and Elizabeth Darcy (d.1617).
It is a Grade II* listed building (listed 28 August 1953; Historic England list entry 1183440).
Declared redundant on 16 June 2002, it was vested in the Churches Conservation Trust on 1 August 2002 and is in its care.
It stands in the churchyard of the present St Dunstan's Church on Church Road, off Malden Road (A2043), Cheam.
w: When old St Dunstan's church in Cheam was demolished in the 1860s, its medieval chancel was spared — and survives as Lumley Chapel, a rare fragment of the original village church. Inside wait grand Elizabethan tombs and a fine plaster ceiling, kept by the Churches Conservation Trust. A medieval room that outlived its church.

hook: Parkland on the Cheam fringe of London where Henry VIII's most extravagant palace once stood, now utterly vanished.
facts: The park is the last surviving part of the Little Park of Nonsuch, Henry VIII's deer-hunting park surrounding the long-gone Nonsuch Palace; it straddles the boundary between the London Borough of Sutton and Epsom & Ewell (Surrey) and is run by a joint management committee of both.

Henry VIII began Nonsuch Palace in 1538 on the site of the demolished village of Cuddington, including its 12th-century church; the palace was unfinished at his death in 1547 and was pulled down around 1682-1683, its materials sold to cover Barbara, Countess of Castlemaine's gambling debts.
The buried palace site is marked by three small stone columns bearing plaques with the building's groundplan.
The park contains Nonsuch Mansion (Nonsuch Park House), built in the mid-18th century and remodelled in Tudor Gothic style by Jeffry Wyatville from 1802-1806; it is Grade II* listed.

Near the western edge of the park survives a low retaining wall, rebuilt in the 19th century using some original Tudor bricks, on the site of the Tudor banqueting house demolished in 1667, later converted into an arboretum.
w: This handsome parkland on the Sutton edge hides one of England's great vanished buildings: Nonsuch Palace, Henry VIII's fantastical 'none such' hunting lodge of gilded plaster and towers, built to outshine the French and gone within 150 years. Nothing of it stands; faint earthworks mark the spot. A palace remembered only in its name.

hook: A deliberately fake brick 'ruin' built in the 2000s to give a new civic square an instant fragment of the past.
facts: The Folly Wall is part of Barking Town Square, a public space designed by muf architecture/art with Allford Hall Monaghan Morris, completed in 2008 as part of a roughly 2.2m-pound regeneration of Barking town centre.

The bare brickwork wall is a deliberate faux ruin: it masks the back-of-house and service yard of an adjacent supermarket while forming a fourth wall to the civic square and a place to sit in the sun.

It is built from architectural salvage bedded into a single-brick-thick wall on a steel frame, and is planted with indigenous species; in resembling a ruin it evokes the red-brick facades of Barking's old buildings.
Adjoining the wall is an arboretum of forty mature trees of sixteen species, lit at night in colours that change with the time of year.
The square sits beside Barking Town Hall at Town Hall Square, off Clockhouse Avenue, IG11.
The scheme was shortlisted for the European Union Prize for Contemporary Architecture (Mies van der Rohe Award).
w: A crumbling, ivy-hung 'ancient ruin' rises beside Barking Town Hall — except it was built in 2008, conjured by the architects muf from reclaimed bricks as a deliberate fake folly for the new town square. Part garden wall, part stage set, part sly joke about heritage. Children clamber over instant history. A ruin with no past at all.

hook: A weathered sarsen block by the Guildhall said to be where seven Anglo-Saxon kings were crowned.
facts: The Coronation Stone is an unhewn block of sarsen (hard sandstone) traditionally held to be the site where Anglo-Saxon kings were consecrated at Kingston.

Documented coronations at Kingston include Aethelstan (925), Eadred (946) and Aethelred the Unready (979); Edward the Elder, Edmund I, Eadwig and Edward the Martyr are also linked to the town, giving the figure of 'seven kings'.
It stands beside the Guildhall on the High Street, where it crosses the Hogsmill River, on a heptagonal plinth inscribed with the kings' names and coronation dates.
It is a Grade I listed structure (Historic England list entry 1080066).

Recovered from the ruins of the old Church of St Mary (which collapsed in 1730) and once used as a mounting block, it was set up on its plinth in the marketplace in 1850 and moved to the Guildhall frontage around 1935.
The name Kingston derives from Old English meaning roughly 'the king's farmstead/settlement'.
w: A weathered lump of sarsen by Kingston's old guildhall claims an outsized history: seven Anglo-Saxon kings, Athelstan and Ethelred the Unready among them, are said to have been crowned upon it in the 900s. Railed and a little forlorn now, it anchors the town's ancient name. England's coronation seat, before the Abbey.

hook: An 1803 smock mill on St Mary's Lane, restored to working order in 2023 after a Dutch millwright rebuilt its cap and sails.
facts: Built in 1803 as a four-storey timber smock mill on a single-storey brick base, for James Nokes of Hunt's Farm.
Grade II* listed (first listed in 1955); regarded as one of the finest surviving smock mills in England.
Commercial corn-milling by the Abraham family ceased in the mid-1930s (the last working brothers retired around 1934-35).
Restored between 2016 and 2023; the cap was shipped to the Netherlands and rebuilt and four new sails fitted by Dutch millwright Willem Dijkstra.
Stands in Windmill Field, a small open space off St Mary's Lane maintained by Havering Council, with a visitor centre added during the restoration.
Now run by the Friends of Upminster Windmill and open to the public on selected days.
w: A handsome white-capped smock mill from 1803 stands improbably amid the Havering streets, its sails turning again after a restoration finished in 2023. For over a century it ground the corn of rural Essex before the suburbs swallowed the fields. Volunteers run open days when the sails sweep the sky. The countryside, refusing to leave.

hook: A reservoir built in the 1830s to feed the canals, now a 170-acre nature reserve and SSSI famed for its breeding great crested grebes.
facts: Formed in 1834-35 by damming the valley of the River Brent to supply water to the Paddington branch of the Grand Junction (now Grand Union) Canal.
Popularly called the Welsh Harp after a tavern of that name; the inn rebuilt in 1859 made the area one of Victorian London's great recreation and entertainment grounds.
Designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest in 1950 - the only SSSI in either Brent or Barnet - covering about 68.6 hectares (170 acres).
Owned and managed by the Canal & River Trust; it straddles the boundary between the boroughs of Brent and Barnet.
Notable chiefly for breeding wetland birds, especially nationally significant numbers of nesting great crested grebes, plus wintering wildfowl and waterside plants.
Over 250 bird species have been recorded at the reservoir over time.
w: Built in the 1830s to feed the canals, this reservoir on the Brent became a Victorian playground of regattas, fairs and a pub that lent it the name 'Welsh Harp.' The crowds are long gone; it's now a tranquil 170-acre nature reserve, loud with dinghies and birdsong. A Victorian leisure lake, gone gently wild. Open water in the suburbs.
